what_it_does: This file is an interpretive architecture and customization-surface map of OpenWebUI 0.9.6, written from the donor/host side of a company fusion. It documents the installed backend subsystems, extension points, APIs, provenance, license constraints, runtime-vs-fork customization boundaries, and an honest assessment of which surfaces are strong or weak for harvesting.
observations: It resolves an earlier suspicion that the installed code was a private fork, verifying stock upstream provenance through bundled CHANGELOG issue/PR references.
It maps three in-process extension surfaces — Pipe, Filter, and Action Functions plus Tools — that share a single DB-stored Python plugin loader.
Plugins are stored as Python strings in database rows and executed in the host process without a sandbox.
It documents a Valves/UserValves configuration pattern where injected parameters are signature-sniffed via inspect.signature.
Channels are described as a relational Slack-style subsystem with AI models as first-class streaming participants through the full chat pipeline.
Voice is described as a stateless backend TTS/STT pair, while the conversational call loop lives in the compiled frontend.
Conversations are shown to have a dual legacy-JSON and normalized-chat_message store that is mid-migration.
Identity includes SCIM, OAuth/OIDC, LDAP, trusted-header SSO, API keys, and a relational AccessGrant table.
The frontend is a compiled SvelteKit bundle, so runtime UI customization is limited to custom.css and loader.js.
The license is BSD-3 plus an anti-branding clause that caps unbranded use at 50 end-users in any rolling 30-day window.
It explicitly separates supported runtime extension surfaces from fork-only changes and flags fragile or version-specific bits.
standouts: The extension model is treated as the most extensively documented fusion surface.
Channels plus AI-as-participant is highlighted as a distinct Beta surface.
The license anti-branding clause is highlighted as a decisive constraint for a branded fusion.
The strong-vs-weak section explicitly lists subsystems to keep and to skip, including the deprecated Pipelines server.
conventions: Uses structured map frontmatter with type, title, subject, version, provenance, posture, and evidence_basis.
Cites evidence as file:line references and live docs.openwebui.com URLs.
Labels major subsystems with an [upstream] provenance tag.
Distinguishes Observed claims from Inferred claims, especially for frontend UI behavior.
Includes a capability-to-mechanism table for direct lookup.
Uses neutral, non-ranking language and explicit fragility flags.
Breaks the convention of a plain reference by being harvest-oriented and evaluative.
concerns: Frontend UI claims are marked Inferred because no Svelte source exists in the venv, so they may be inaccurate.
Provenance verification relies on the bundled CHANGELOG attributions, which are assumed truthful.
Fragility flags are asserted without quantified impact or reproduction steps.
Strong-vs-weak assessments are subjective despite the neutral framing.
The map is pinned to OpenWebUI 0.9.6 and may become stale quickly.
notes: A harvest-oriented architecture map of stock OpenWebUI 0.9.6 that catalogs backend extension points, flags what is runtime-vs-fork-only, and highlights the license branding cap as the main fusion constraint.
questions: What exact criteria define 'supported-extension' versus 'fork-only' beyond the compiled frontend?
How was the live docs.openwebui.com content sampled and on what date?
What is the company's target substrate against which the strong/weak assessment is judged?
Are there any local environment-specific configurations outside the documented venv path?
What is the intended scope of the company fusion — selective harvest, full integration, or UI replacement?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the stated purpose is to map what is in the installed code and live docs, flag fragile/version-specific bits, and distinguish supported-extension from fork-only surfaces, and the file does exactly that while adding provenance resolution, mechanism tables, and harvest-oriented assessment.
apparent_themes: Separability over monolith — value lies in backend subsystems rather than the compiled frontend.
Evidence-based mapping — claims tied to file:line or documentation URLs.
Honest fragility — explicit beta, legacy, and version-specific flags.
License-aware harvesting — legal constraints treated as engineering gates.
Runtime extension preference — favor DB-driven, no-rebuild surfaces.
intention_signals: The file appears to be trying to give a company fusion team a decision-ready donor-side dossier: which OpenWebUI subsystems are reusable, which require forking or licensing, and which should be skipped, while correcting mistaken assumptions about fork-private features.
novelty: It is an interpretive architecture map written as a fusion ledger entry, combining provenance investigation, capability-to-mechanism lookup, explicit strong/weak valuation, and license-constraint analysis in one document rather than a conventional design doc or API reference.
contribution: It contributes a curated inventory of OpenWebUI backend extension points and integration seams — Functions, Tools, Channels, RAG, identity, and multi-connection model plumbing — together with provenance, fragility, and license framing that a fusion planner can use to decide what to adopt, wrap, or replace.
summary_for_embedding: An interpretive architecture map of stock OpenWebUI 0.9.6 documenting its backend subsystems, extension surfaces, APIs, provenance, runtime-vs-fork customization boundaries, and harvest-oriented strong/weak assessment for a company fusion.
intention_for_embedding: To give the company fusion team a donor-side reference for deciding which OpenWebUI surfaces are reusable, which are constrained by license or frontend compilation, and which should be avoided.
